### Standards for Dried and Dehydrated Foods:

Drying and dehydration are ancient food preservation methods that reduce water content to inhibit microbial growth.[14](https://agriculture.institute/food-microbiology-cpo/drying-and-dehydration-food-preservation/), [15](https://vertexaisearch.cloud.google.com/grounding-api-redirect/AUZIYQGvJldkZgTMHFgqUyWClMboTnaVO4i5sB1TNluTEJNhjB1YWohUY4FUtkHb8_QrABmRVHG0WN94vpLN_gPegzkHoHmjwRBU-Ppr40fbta5YFHL80Vh-RRfTM6qCjvMylA02a1AOF5RpxccG3hcQioqxWYwCbjihKg==), [16](https://www.pubs.ext.vt.edu/348/348-597/348-597.html) The FDA considers foods with a water activity below 0.85 as shelf-stable.[14](https://agriculture.institute/food-microbiology-cpo/drying-and-dehydration-food-preservation/) The term "dehydrated foods" technically refers to products with no more than 2.5% water on a dry weight basis, while "dried foods" can have more.[14](https://agriculture.institute/food-microbiology-cpo/drying-and-dehydration-food-preservation/)

*   **Specific Products & Percentages:** FSIS guidelines provide compositional requirements for certain dried meat and poultry products. For example, for dried products containing poultry, percentages are computed based on the moist cooked chicken in the ready-to-serve product.[18](https://www.fsis.usda.gov/sites/default/files/import/Labeling-Policy-Book.pdf) The USDA's Agricultural Marketing Service (AMS) has commodity specifications for dried fruits, with updates occurring over the years (e.g., January 2021 for dried fruit, February 2021 for dried sweet cherries).[19](https://www.ams.usda.gov/selling-food/product-specs)

### Standards for Frozen Foods:

Freezing preserves freshness and quality by preventing microbial growth, though it does not kill bacteria.[20](https://www.ncrfsma.org/files/page/files/ncr_frozen_and_dehydrated_foods_1.pdf), [21](https://www.fsis.usda.gov/food-safety/safe-food-handling-and-preparation/food-safety-basics/freezing-and-food-safety) Foods should be stored at 0°F (-18°C) or lower to maintain quality and safety.

[21](https://www.fsis.usda.gov/food-safety/safe-food-handling-and-preparation/food-safety-basics/freezing-and-food-safety), [22](https://www.fda.gov/media/74435/download)*   **Labeling:** FDA policy dictates that labels for frozen foods should include descriptive terms like "frozen" to prevent misrepresentation, especially if a thawed product could be mistaken for fresh. *[17](https://www.fda.gov/regulatory-information/search-fda-guidance-documents/cpg-sec-562450-identity-foods-use-terms-such-fresh-frozen-dried-canned-etc)   **Specific Products & Percentages:** The Egg Products Inspection Act of 1970 brought mandatory continuous inspection for frozen egg products. U[18](https://www.fsis.usda.gov/sites/default/files/import/Labeling-Policy-Book.pdf)SDA AMS also issues commodity specifications for frozen fruits (e.g., February 2022).

[19](https://www.ams.usda.gov/selling-food/product-specs)### Standards for Chilled Foods:

Chilled foods are subject to strict temperature controls to prevent pathogen growth. The FDA Food Code, a model for state and local food safety regulations, provides scientifically validated temperature parameters.

[23](https://www.fda.gov/food/retail-food-protection/fda-food-code), [24](https://www.sensoscientific.com/fda-temperature-monitoring-compliance-food-safety/)*   **Temperature Control:** Since 2017, the FDA Food Code has stipulated cold holding temperatures of 41°F (5°C) or below, decreased from 45°F. Hot [24](https://www.sensoscientific.com/fda-temperature-monitoring-compliance-food-safety/), [25](https://static1.squarespace.com/static/5c4085e585ede1f50f94a4b9/t/648770317757de56cb414075/1761067903416/FDA%2BFood%2BCode%2BInfo%2BSession%2BPowerpoint.pdf)holding temperatures changed from a minimum of 140°F to 135°F (57°C) or above. Food safety and quality standards in the United States, governed primarily by the U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA), the Food Safety and Inspection Service (FSIS), and the Food and Drug Administration (FDA), have undergone significant evolution between 1959 and 2023. ### Key Regulatory Bodies and Their Evolving Roles:

*   **FDA (Food and Drug Administration):** The FDA is responsible for the safety of most foods, with the exception of meat, poultry, and certain egg products. Key legislative acts such as the **Food Additives Amendment of 1958** required manufacturers to prove the safety of food additives and led to the establishment of a "Generally Recognized As Safe (GRAS)" list.[1](https://www.fsis.usda.gov/about-fsis/history), [2](https://trust20.co/blog/food-safety-regulations-history), [3](https://gobigread.wisc.edu/2019/05/americas-food-policy-timeline) The **Color Additives Amendment of 1960** further mandated the FDA to approve color additives.[2](https://trust20.co/blog/food-safety-regulations-history) A pivotal change came with the **FDA Food Safety Modernization Act (FSMA) of 2011**, which provided the FDA with new enforcement authorities, emphasized preventive controls, and aimed to hold imported foods to the same standards as domestic products.[4](https://www.sutori.com/en/story/milestones-in-food-regulations--PXvJm94PtDU4Up6wYfvBiCbM), [5](https://www.fda.gov/about-fda/fda-history/milestones-us-food-and-drug-law) The FDA also establishes "Standards of Identity" (SOIs) for many foods, detailing required and optional ingredients and sometimes their proportions.[6](https://www.fda.gov/food/nutrition-food-labeling-and-critical-foods/standards-identity-food) For instance, SOIs for fruit preserves and jams require a minimum amount of fruit.[6](https://www.fda.gov/food/nutrition-food-labeling-and-critical-foods/standards-identity-food)
*   **USDA and FSIS (Food Safety and Inspection Service):** The USDA's inspection authority for meat products dates back to the late 19th century, with the **Federal Meat Inspection Act (FMIA) of 1906** strengthening requirements for sanitary conditions and inspection of meat in interstate commerce.[7](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK235649/), [1](https://www.fsis.usda.gov/about-fsis/history), [8](https://vertexaisearch.cloud.google.com/grounding-api-redirect/AUZIYQF_6cYqWn3Br97DRJ-tvlh4r6DNT2rwgSZG0Q7BjHY2gqtDTAkuKmfSHMjUKfi0d58rXkVA8WHJuKK1ECMBxOPF0MPd-o1REXm-KrukcQz0A9KXI2Ub8srJx6VKznY=) The **Poultry Products Inspection Act of 1957** extended continuous inspection to poultry products.[1](https://www.fsis.usda.gov/about-fsis/history) A significant amendment, the **Wholesome Meat Act of 1967**, gave the USDA authority to regulate transporters, renderers, cold storage warehouses, and animal-food manufacturers, also making ante-mortem inspection mandatory.[7](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK235649/), [1](https://www.fsis.usda.gov/about-fsis/history), [9](https://rvs.umn.edu/Uploads/EducationalMaterials/4934157a-2ccd-43b6-b1ae-9003de307b8f.pdf) In 1970, the **Egg Products Inspection Act (EPIA)** mandated continuous inspection for liquid, frozen, and dried egg products.[1](https://www.fsis.usda.gov/about-fsis/history) The **Food Safety and Quality Service (FSQS)** was created in 1977, which was then reorganized and renamed the **Food Safety and Inspection Service (FSIS)** in 1981, becoming responsible for ensuring the safety, wholesomeness, and accurate labeling of meat, poultry, and egg products.[1](https://www.fsis.usda.gov/about-fsis/history), [8](https://vertexaisearch.cloud.google.com/grounding-api-redirect/AUZIYQF_6cYqWn3Br97DRJ-tvlh4r6DNT2rwgSZG0Q7BjHY2gqtDTAkuKmfSHMjUKfi0d58rXkVA8WHJuKK1ECMBxOPF0MPd-o1REXm-KrukcQz0A9KXI2Ub8srJx6VKznY=) A major shift in regulatory philosophy for FSIS occurred with the **Pathogen Reduction/HACCP Systems rule in 1996**, moving towards science-based inspection focused on preventing and reducing microbial pathogens.[1](https://www.fsis.usda.gov/about-fsis/history), [9](https://rvs.umn.edu/Uploads/EducationalMaterials/4934157a-2ccd-43b6-b1ae-9003de307b8f.pdf) FSIS continues to update its guidelines and HACCP models for various products, including ready-to-eat fermented, salt-cured, and dried products, with new models and guidelines released in 2023 and 2025.[10](https://www.fsis.usda.gov/policy/fsis-guidelines), [11](https://www.fsis.usda.gov/guidelines/2023-0002), [12](https://www.fsis.usda.gov/news-events/news-press-releases/constituent-update-august-8-2025), [13](https://www.fsis.usda.gov/news-events/news-press-releases/constituent-update-june-27-2025)

### Standards for Dried and Dehydrated Foods:

Drying and dehydration are ancient food preservation methods that reduce water content to inhibit microbial growth.[14](https://agriculture.institute/food-microbiology-cpo/drying-and-dehydration-food-preservation/), [15](https://vertexaisearch.cloud.google.com/grounding-api-redirect/AUZIYQGvJldkZgTMHFgqUyWClMboTnaVO4i5sB1TNluTEJNhjB1YWohUY4FUtkHb8_QrABmRVHG0WN94vpLN_gPegzkHoHmjwRBU-Ppr40fbta5YFHL80Vh-RRfTM6qCjvMylA02a1AOF5RpxccG3hcQioqxWYwCbjihKg==), [16](https://www.pubs.ext.vt.edu/348/348-597/348-597.html) The FDA considers foods with a water activity below 0.85 as shelf-stable.[14](https://agriculture.institute/food-microbiology-cpo/drying-and-dehydration-food-preservation/) The term "dehydrated foods" technically refers to products with no more than 2.5% water on a dry weight basis, while "dried foods" can have more.[14](https://agriculture.institute/food-microbiology-cpo/drying-and-dehydration-food-preservation/)

*   **Labeling:** FDA guidance states that dried or dehydrated foods should be labeled with a designation including one of these words, unless the name (e.g., "raisins") already indicates a dried product.[17](https://www.fda.gov/regulatory-information/search-fda-guidance-documents/cpg-sec-562450-identity-foods-use-terms-such-fresh-frozen-dried-canned-etc) "Freeze dried" is also an acceptable and more informative designation.[17](https://www.fda.gov/regulatory-information/search-fda-guidance-documents/cpg-sec-562450-identity-foods-use-terms-such-fresh-frozen-dried-canned-etc)
*   **Specific Products & Percentages:** FSIS guidelines provide compositional requirements for certain dried meat and poultry products. For example, for dried products containing poultry, percentages are computed based on the moist cooked chicken in the ready-to-serve product.[18](https://www.fsis.usda.gov/sites/default/files/import/Labeling-Policy-Book.pdf) The USDA's Agricultural Marketing Service (AMS) has commodity specifications for dried fruits, with updates occurring over the years (e.g., January 2021 for dried fruit, February 2021 for dried sweet cherries).[19](https://www.ams.usda.gov/selling-food/product-specs)

### Standards for Frozen Foods:

Freezing preserves freshness and quality by preventing microbial growth, though it does not kill bacteria.[20](https://www.ncrfsma.org/files/page/files/ncr_frozen_and_dehydrated_foods_1.pdf), [21](https://www.fsis.usda.gov/food-safety/safe-food-handling-and-preparation/food-safety-basics/freezing-and-food-safety) Foods should be stored at 0°F (-18°C) or lower to maintain quality and safety.

[21](https://www.fsis.usda.gov/food-safety/safe-food-handling-and-preparation/food-safety-basics/freezing-and-food-safety), [22](https://www.fda.gov/media/74435/download)*   **Labeling:** FDA policy dictates that labels for frozen foods should include descriptive terms like "frozen" to prevent misrepresentation, especially if a thawed product could be mistaken for fresh. T[17](https://www.fda.gov/regulatory-information/search-fda-guidance-documents/cpg-sec-562450-identity-foods-use-terms-such-fresh-frozen-dried-canned-etc)he term "frozen fresh" or "fresh frozen" can be used for foods that were fresh when frozen.
*[17](https://www.fda.gov/regulatory-information/search-fda-guidance-documents/cpg-sec-562450-identity-foods-use-terms-such-fresh-frozen-dried-canned-etc)   **Specific Products & Percentages:** The Egg Products Inspection Act of 1970 brought mandatory continuous inspection for frozen egg products. F[1](https://www.fsis.usda.gov/about-fsis/history)SIS has guidelines for "Frozen products labeled as 'dinner' or 'supper'" requiring them to weigh at least 10 ounces and contain at least 3 components (meat, poultry, cheese, eggs, vegetables, fruit, potatoes, rice, or other cereal-based products). U[18](https://www.fsis.usda.gov/sites/default/files/import/Labeling-Policy-Book.pdf)SDA AMS also issues commodity specifications for frozen fruits (e.g., February 2022).

[19](https://www.ams.usda.gov/selling-food/product-specs)### Standards for Chilled Foods:

Chilled foods are subject to strict temperature controls to prevent pathogen growth. The FDA Food Code, a model for state and local food safety regulations, provides scientifically validated temperature parameters.

[23](https://www.fda.gov/food/retail-food-protection/fda-food-code), [24](https://www.sensoscientific.com/fda-temperature-monitoring-compliance-food-safety/)*   **Temperature Control:** Since 2017, the FDA Food Code has stipulated cold holding temperatures of 41°F (5°C) or below, decreased from 45°F. Hot [24](https://www.sensoscientific.com/fda-temperature-monitoring-compliance-food-safety/), [25](https://static1.squarespace.com/static/5c4085e585ede1f50f94a4b9/t/648770317757de56cb414075/1761067903416/FDA%2BFood%2BCode%2BInfo%2BSession%2BPowerpoint.pdf)holding temperatures changed from a minimum of 140°F to 135°F (57°C) or above. Cooling[24](https://www.sensoscientific.com/fda-temperature-monitoring-compliance-food-safety/), [25](https://static1.squarespace.com/static/5c4085e585ede1f50f94a4b9/t/648770317757de56cb414075/1761067903416/FDA%2BFood%2BCode%2BInfo%2BSession%2BPowerpoint.pdf) parameters also became more defined, requiring food to cool from 135°F to 70°F within 2 hours, and then to 41°F within an additional 4 hours. The FDA de[24](https://www.sensoscientific.com/fda-temperature-monitoring-compliance-food-safety/)signates the range between 41°F and 135°F as the "temperature danger zone" where pathogens multiply most rapidly.
*   **FSIS S[24](https://www.sensoscientific.com/fda-temperature-monitoring-compliance-food-safety/)tabilization Guidelines:** FSIS provides stabilization guidelines for meat and poultry products, which include time and temperature cooling parameters. In the 1980s, prescriptive cooling regulations were introduced for cooked beef, roast beef, and cooked corned beef due to outbreaks. With the imp[26](https://www.fsis.usda.gov/sites/default/files/media_file/2021-12/Appendix-B.pdf)lementation of HACCP in 1996, these prescriptive regulations were converted to "Safe Harbors" within Appendix B, offering establishments guidance for cooling processes.

In summary,[26](https://www.fsis.usda.gov/sites/default/files/media_file/2021-12/Appendix-B.pdf) the period from 1959 to 2023 has seen a significant shift in U.S. food standards from basic sanitation and inspection to a comprehensive, science-based approach emphasizing preventive controls and traceability. While early regulations established fundamental safety requirements and some compositional standards, later legislation like FSMA and the implementation of HACCP systems have focused on preventing foodborne illnesses through rigorous monitoring, risk assessment, and detailed guidelines, including specific temperature and compositional requirements for dried, dehydrated, frozen, and chilled foods.
